2. Training and Treating Sales People Right - Sales League.mp4

We love life. I've been married about to hit 13 years, and my son struck out six batters today. I was watching during Jeremy's session. I was paying attention, taking notes, but I had the game app open. And just watching him crush it at the same time. So I'm a dad through and through, first and foremost.
Jake is as well, him and his beautiful wife and his two kids. Twenty-five years I've been a record producer. Not a sales trainer. Twenty-five years I've traveled the globe, almost every major country. I got more stamps in my passport. I bet I could totally jack with Jay-Z's passport, as he says in his song. But I got to travel the globe making records. I've played, I've produced, and I loved it. Three years ago, I went from making records to crushing records and sales. How? I still have no freaking idea. I've distilled it down. I've written it down. I haven't hypnotized myself yet, but I've still tried to figure it out through and through. How is this possible? And what I've come up with is simply this belief.
Culture who I am, who I have destined myself to be, and what I believe to be true always works. I came into a situation where, uh, big, big, you know, coaching consulting space. I don't know much about that space, to be honest with you, because I didn't grow up in it. I don't do the ads and all that crap. I just know how to make people money, that's about it. This person was pretty big, did pretty well when I came in. They were probably doing, I don't know, million, half in a month when I came in. They started doing two and a half, three million a month. In sales, it was great. All these people were great at sales. They knew the tactics. They knew the structure.
They knew exactly how to slice and dice you. And I was like, 'Give me your fucking money.' And they did it. So how did that work? I didn't have the tactics. I didn't have the structure. How was I able to actually do this? Honestly, I simply believe that I just had more conviction than the other person on the phone. And when I didn't have conviction because I didn't know what I was doing, you know what I had? I had something that Jeremy talked about earlier. I had a high enough pain point. As a musician, I was doing really well. For a musician, so I'm putting those quotes around what 'doing really well' is. You know, I'd bring in 100 and grand a year 120 grand a year, and you know, for a musician, that wasn't too bad.
That is better than the average person. But when you have a lot of student loans and the college that I went to and yada yada, stack up, $ 120 grand, let's just be honest. I don't care if you don't have student loans. It's just not a lot of money, right? Can anybody agree to that? Okay, cool. Because his room seems like there's a lot of money in here. I heard some dude bought a yacht that seems cool. I bought a truck and um, and I liked it. Uh, but what I found out is I had such high pain point that I wanted more for myself and more for my family. My parents were poor. I love them. Pastors, they're absolutely amazing, but they were poor. Their parents were poor.
Their parents were poor and their parents were poor. I got to the point where I was like, I don't want that life. so what does it take and somebody simply said well what's your game plan I said, I'm going to make a bigger record and it's going to hit and it's going to blow up and I'm going to make money. And they're like, how are you going to do that? How are you going to make sure that that record does that? How are you going to make more songs that do that? How are you going to do that? And what I found out in about a 15 minute conversation on a lake while smoking a very fine cigar, I had no game plan. I had no understanding how this was actually going to work.
I also found out that I had zero control. So what I learned was if I got into sales, I could put more kerosene on the fire. Listen to what I'm saying. Kerosene burns cleaner and longer than gasoline. Gasoline is dirty. It burns, but it's over quickly. What I needed was kerosene that would give me full control of how much money I could make. Through that time, I started crushing records and I got into that. What I realized was this was a repeatable process. And everything that I learned through the church world, through the freaking music world, through managing people, because I had to manage. Management so I had to put schedules together and I had to pull people together that are a bunch of lazy, emotional people— anybody ever met a musician by the way, probably the most lazy, emotional people.
Worse than SDRs. Absolutely the worst. And they are lazy and they're emotional. And you have to find a way to make them wake up, take their money, get fed, food when they walk into catering some arena, and still at the exact same time say, 'Please go play and do your job.' And don't be drunk. or don't be high out of your mind. That's very hard to get a bunch of idiots together. Come to find out, salespeople are just like dumb musicians. You just have to know how to rally them up. So that's what I want to talk to you about today. Sound good? Sound good? All right, let's go. This was my response. How cool is that? Profile pic right. Metrics matter, but they only tell a portion of the story. I know some of you marketers, right? Now are probably like cringing in your seats and you're like, 'Nah, my numbers. They line up. I've got the formulas and they add up and they're ready to go The power is when you can track the tangible metrics and the hidden metrics at the exact same time. I got a friend. For all, you know, intense purposes, his name is going to be Jimmy. Jimmy came from nothing, had nothing, had no one to help him, technically was adopted. Um, Dad was a Mexican cartel member who was deported in front of him. He got adopted by the most white of white people and was raised by them.
Then that family split up. He had nothing. He got into sales. Figured himself out. Figured out how to pull himself up with his bootstraps. We fast forward a couple years. Jimmy is doing quite well. Not great. Let's say he's doing 10 to 15K a month, right? For a salesperson, that's pretty solid, right? Over a holiday season, I have an open door policy with anyone who works with me. You can call me, you can hit me up. If I'm with my wife and my family, I'm just not going to answer it, and you can deal with it. But I'll hit you back. I'll take care of your call and I'll take care of you. Do you think it messes with your psyche? You think it messes with your belief, your conviction? Okay. So two weeks in after I let it crash and you have to let it crash to a degree I sat down with them. And I said, hey, what's going on? He's, oh man, this marketing, blah, blah, blah. What do you think sales guys say when you ask them what's going on? Anybody know? They sound like the old guy trying to fix a carburetor with a cigarette hanging out of his mouth. I was like, okay, cool, cool, cool. But what I realized was this: when I had to start addressing what I call the hidden metrics. So what do you do when all your numbers look good?
Touch points, lead to book, book call to show rate, consults completed, LTV, AOV, days to close, but yet your team... Can't seem to close a damn door. What do you do? How do you reconcile it? Well, you go to the hidden metrics. What are the hidden metrics? Well, the hidden metrics are the intangibles that can't be measured with numbers, but they do massively affect the metrics that can be, which obviously affects your bottom lines. I had a good CMO at this job that I was closing at. And he would always say, 'Look at the numbers, look at the numbers, look at the numbers.' And I was like, 'Listen here, homie, I'm grown enough to tell you this.' I can tell you one thing to be true: You can look at numbers through and through all day long.
They are only going to tell a portion of the story. So what is the other part of the story? That's the hidden metrics that I call. If I can tell. Let me say it like this. I call the hidden metrics because they are basically the metrics that most business owners, leaders, sales managers, sadly enough, they just don't want to look at. They don't want to pay attention to. They don't want to actually engage in. We're going to talk about why that is. I'm gonna prove it. I hope this isn't too early to post a picture of Bill Cosby. Still love the guy. Obviously, he made some bad decisions. I'm not here to throw stones, but I mean, the Jell-O commercial is classic, right? Am I the oldest person in this freaking room right now?
Nobody knows about the... you know come on, my man in the back—damn, where'd you get those biceps at homie? That's oh, I didn't— did anybody else see him walk in, freaking bright orange ass shirt, big ass biceps. What about your sales numbers? No, I'm just kidding. So here's the proof: it's in the pudding. He probably has better than me. If you have big biceps like that, most likely your sales crush too. If I were to ask you the age-old question, 'who hasn't been picked on?' Have you been picked on yet? No. How's your business doing? What are you going to think about to answer that question? The what? Who else would think about that? If I asked you, 'hey, how's your business doing? What are you thinking about?' Numbers.
How much? Revenue is funny. That we talk about revenue and profits, the only damn thing that matters anybody else? No cool. Maybe that's just me. I like profit because that's what goes into my actual bank account. Uh, so the hidden metrics they're less obvious. They're potentially hidden bottlenecks impacting your business and how it's doing. So. Do we want to know what they are? Nope. I told you, y'all got to talk. I'm not afraid of y'all. I'm not afraid of your day dates, your Rolexes, your Patagonias. I'm not afraid of any of that shit. Do y'all want to know what they actually are? All right, let's go. Come on. So the hidden metrics. Are the health of your team. They're the health of your team. These hidden metrics are not found in ads.
They're not found in funnels. They're not found in all those damn spreadsheets you marketing motherfuckers put out and make me look at. You got all the tabs at the bottom and your color. Code them and then they got the things and then you click them. It's like, well, look here— this number right here is 0. 055. Says you should have more clothes. I'm like, that's a freaking spreadsheet that doesn't mean shit to me. That's just a number. Again, I support the numbers. We look at numbers. Don't get mad. I totally believe in numbers. But the hidden metrics, what I'm talking about today is about the health of your business. The hidden metrics are found in the way that you, everybody say it with me, care. Connect. Cultivate.
Say it again— the way that you care? The way that you connect. And the way that you cultivate. Come on. They are found in your 'give a damn.' And if your 'give a damn' is busted, then your sales are going to reflect it. So I want to help you get back to 'give a damn.' I want to show you a simple process. I'm going to give you something to actually walk away with, an actual tangible principle or application that you can apply. It's going to show you how to systematize managing people. How you can care, connect, and cultivate with them so that they can get back to the best version of themselves and operating at maximum capacity, whatever that is for them, and we could talk for days about what true maximum capacity is.
Sometimes, what I have learned and this is a little caveat: some people don't want to be an 'a player Yo, I will read every Debbie Goggins book that comes out, but my ass ain't ever run in no hundred mile race. I don't desire to push my brain that far from a physical capacity standpoint. I don't know. Maybe one day I will. I just don't think I will. What I have learned in my years of managing just people, and it's very much translating into sales professionals, some of them want greatness, but their desire and level of greatness might not align with yours. Can we be okay with that? Can we still achieve what we're trying to achieve if person A doesn't hit at 100%? Anybody? Can we?
Can you still get what you're trying to get? Could I get two people that crush at 60% and still give me the results of me at 100? Yeah. This is not a trick question. This is pretty much basic math. Is 2 plus 2 4? All right. I could have sworn I was here with people who did math for a living. All right. Here's the caveat and disclaimer. I'm going to read it here. This is a simple system. Okay, like you're part of the team, maybe you're fractional, and you're contributing, you're doing all you can, because marketing is perfect. Right? You guys never mess it up, you always crush it. Right? Marketing, every ad works. Come on, all the ads are perfect. Copy never gets you know, never gets worn out. You can use the same picture for six years.
But let's say sales is not going well, or a sales team member you're seeing them disengage. Because all of my marketing guys were always in the sales channels, they were watching what was going on, and they were tracking stuff. What you could do, if you noticed somebody was starting to trail off, But you didn't have direct control and you didn't want to step on the sales manager's toes or anything like that. You could go the opposite route and hit them up in Slack. Hey, I just wanted to tell you, I think you're doing awesome. It makes me want to work harder for my job because of how hard you're working. Maybe you say, 'Hey, name,' I just wanted to let you know, if there's ever anything that you need from me, let me know.
We're really trying hard to dial in new creatives right now. And however we can assist you, could you maybe, I'll take any feedback you can give on how we're doing. Now, do you really want the feedback of some 20-year-old schmuck that doesn't actually know what they're doing? No, that's not the point. The point is how do we pull the maximum capacity out of every person that we're in contact with. Let me ask you this. Is anybody in this because they don't actually care about people? That's right. This is like Jeremy, although probably the craziest human being I've met. I've only known him now for two months. I mean, I've never met anybody as tall as him and as just crazy and bonkers as he is. And but what I have pulled back from two months.
He actually cares about people. You know how I know? We're not in a hotel room right now. We're in his home. How many other people you know that are big dicking it, big dogging, sorry— that just, you know, at a next level, let you come into their home? That you stay in their home. But you eat their food. Let you meet their dogs and their fiancé. And how many, anybody else know anybody that's just being willy-nilly come on in? I don't know— just as a reminder, because I'm an outside person because I'm new, so I have fresh perspective. If you've been in here for a while, make sure that you re-up your conviction and your belief in the program that you're in.
Because I've been a part of a lot of them and they're not this welcoming, they're not this kind. Why I'm... harping so hard on this is because I believe there is money sitting in your pipelines, and all it takes is one message to a salesperson to say, 'Hey, I believe in you Hey, I think you can do this. Hey, I'm here for you. Create an open door policy with your team members so that they feel like they mean something. I truly believe. I believe people will do whatever the hell you tell them to do. I really do believe that if you can create a conducive enough environment that makes it about them. Does this make sense what I'm saying? I haven't lost anybody, right?
If you can create such a conducive environment where people feel like they matter, that, what's that old saying? They don't care how much you know until they care how much you care. Do you know the names? Of your team members, spouses, kids? Do you know what they're interested in? I just found out one of my team members, because I do this thing where I find out more and more about them. I do this thing where I found out that one of our guys was a black belt. The dude's like 20. years old. I'm like, when did you get that? Do they pass them out now and you don't have to earn them? I was just so shocked by that. I was like, you're 22 years old. I was 22 years old.
I just figured out how to wipe my ass and drive. So I was just shocked by this. But my point is this: deposits and withdrawals, deposits and withdrawals. We wanna have more deposits than we do withdrawals. Y'all understand that analogy, correct? Everybody in here likes making money. So how many deposits are we making into our team members day in and day out? The people around us. One day you are going to get this and it's going to hit so hard home with you or maybe you're all perfect right now and you are the best leaders that we've ever seen and we should be at your mastermind. But my point is this: one day you're gonna get this and then you're gonna start to see, oh my God, I could do this with the people I also love about them.
The people I care about. You know, once a week I sit down with my kids and I ask them, 'How can Dad do a better job? Tell me what Dad did wrong this week and how I can improve.' Yo. And my son, he does not hold back. My son will whoop my ass so hard. And he'd be like, 'You know what, Dad? I just felt like this week you were on your phone during dinner time too much.' I'm like. 'Making money to pay for your jiu-jitsu lessons, you little jerk!' But then I have to be like, 'Yes, sir.' I hear what you're saying. Thank you for that feedback. I received your feedback and daddy will do a better job at engaging with you at dinnertime.
Imagine what we did if we had that same level of care and compassion for the other individuals that were making us money. Or could be making us more money because it's just sitting there. I'm going to keep moving. Everybody good, right? All right. I love the feedback. Pro tip. Pay attention to how your team members are responding inside of your communication channels, meetings, and any other form of communication lanes. Quickest way to see a red flag is to watch the lack of engagement. Communication are possible. Here's a big one. Sarcastic remarks, shots, jabs. Listen, in sales. There are a lot of cultures out there where they kind of like bust each other's balls and it's kind of the thing to do. Honestly, I tell you to watch out for that stuff.
Because it also could be masked. Oh, that culture, that's just how people are. And people are actually lashing out. People are actually like, 'Oh, this culture, if no one really cares, then I'm just going to be a dick.' I don't want that. I like people being savages, by the way. I'm a damn savage. I'm all for that. I will crush anybody. Right now, can I use you as an example, Jordan? Okay. I met this guy last night. I literally think the world of him. I'm about to go buy all of his products on the website. I'm like, I'm just going to freaking buy them and see what happens. I'm going to learn them and use them. Why? Because he's crushing it. But prove my savage point, I believe I'm a better closer than him.
I believe I'm better clothes than anybody in this damn room. Because my belief has to live at that place. So I'm asking myself, how do I teach people how to think like that? But I promise you this, there's nothing that he couldn't ask me from this moment forward that I wouldn't give him. If he called me tomorrow and we just met, he said, 'Bro, I don't know. But you're the only number I could remember. Can you give me 30? I'm in jail. I'm like, yeah, you're going to pay me back, but here it comes. Because people matter more than anything else. And I promise you this: if you can learn to invest into your people, to give to your people, to serve your people, they're going to serve you so much cash up on a silver platter, you won't know how to spend it.' Uh, how do we know the hidden metrics are off?
Uh, some more questions, helping team members out, or fighting for themselves— are they burning dirty energy versus clean energy? I'm about to hit this. Uh, showing up on time, early, willing to go the extra mile, happy, satisfied— are they having fun outside of work? When you're asking for wins, are you hearing them talk about? Oh, I went and did this. Oh, me and my spouse did this. Oh, my boys and this— we hit this trip. If you're not hearing that stuff, that's a telltale sign. And I'll explain that here in a second. If they answer no to any of these, they're a red flag. Okay. So I want to show you how to do these hidden metrics. I need to show you how to care, connect, and cultivate.
Wrapped up into one example, let's hop back into Jimmy's story. Y'all remember Jimmy, right? Little Jimmy, yeah, Little Jimmy's cool man. I like Jimmy, I like these pictures, by the way. This is probably my dad's side coming out, pulling off these stupid uh Google pictures. No, I don't have a cool account to like all the stock photos that you guys creatives have. I just rip them and you know uh shift command for them so uh best of luck. So uh Jimmy. He hops in and we started chatting. I had to work on his hidden metrics. At first, we have that meeting. He's like, 'Oh, Josh, it's this, it's that, it's this, it's that, that's what's going on.' It's the metrics, it's this. Okay, cool.
We hit all of the tangible metrics that you believe why you're not closing deals, like I always do. Here's a little trick for y'all. If you're about to rip somebody a new one. Tell them the hardcore truth— hit them where they you know, like right between the eyes. Ask permission first. Ask permission. Hey, Jimmy, would you be opposed if I just gave you my honest thoughts on everything that you told me back? Yeah, man. Sure. You can say whatever you want, whatever you want. Okay. But just so you know, this is going to hurt. I'm going to step on your toes. I'm going to offend you, but I promise you it's from a place of love. Are you still okay with me giving you the hardcore honest truth? Yeah, go ahead.
Boom. That's when I hit him. My friend, you are burning dark energy. You popped off for a whole month because you were burning dark energy, because you were pissed, sad, depressed about what happened. That gave you dirty energy. Dirty, okay? You can burn that for a while. Prime example: anybody ever heard a story about somebody who had a chip on their shoulder— Adele. Can I use music references? Is that okay? Okay. So Adele, when she actually fell in love with somebody who she was going to marry, producers were so distraught because they're like, she won't write any more sad songs— which is her hits and how she makes money. They thought they were cutting her damn hair off like she was Samsonite.
That's how much people believe in dark energy, dirty energy, it will burn it's like gasoline, it's going to burn it's going to blaze a fire, but it will be short-lived. Okay. So with Jimmy, I had to show him how to get back to burning clean energy. Why are you doing this? What's going on? Let's talk for real. We work through that. You know what he does? He pops off, finishes out, February, strong has an amazing March, and here's the byproduct. You ready? Because I cared about Jimmy. One team member, he directly affected anybody. Ever notice that one team member can affect all the other team members? We ended up popping off for one of our best quarters ever. All, not because what? Not because I told him how to handle an objection.
Oh, not because I told him how to deal with the timing objection or the spousal objection, or I told him how to really stick it to him in hard clothes. Because I found out why. Was he operating the way that he was and what would it look like if he chose to operate in a way that was more conducive for success for what he wanted long term? Then that directly affected everybody else. The energy shifted and pivoted. And guess what? The company made more money. Now imagine if I only had stayed on the metrics. The tangibles, show rate, how many touch points you got. You didn't call that lead enough. You didn't do that. I'm not knocking those things. Those things are 100%. But again, it is a portion of the story.
Y'all still with me? So! I gotta go through this way quicker. I want to show you all a step-by-step process of how we actually do this. Okay. There's enough people in the world. 7 billion people, 3 . 5 of them have the internet access. I can make money. Even if you teach myself.
Sound good? All right. One-to-one sync ups— this is what we call them— when they win, you win. One-to-one meetings are game changers for sales leaders. Again, this is a portion of it. I don't have time to tell you everything that we do, but I want to give you a tangible principle that you can walk away with and say, 'I'm going to implement this' and 'I'm going to try it to make sure that it is working.' They perfect the chance to truly connect with each team member and learn their ambitions and tackle any obstacles. Again, practically, this is what it's going to look like when we're running one-to-one sales meetings. First thing I'm going to do when I get a new team member is to make sure that I have their professional and personal goals and KPIs for each team member.
I want to know how much money you want to make, I want to know why you want to make the money, I want to know what vacations you want to go on. I want to know everything. I want to know that you want to order at this restaurant or experience this thing. I want it all. We normally get this information at the top of the year. We'll help everyone. We walk them through. We have a whole process of how you walk through. Hey, how do you know what you want, what you want? Because most of the time when you ask somebody, 'Hey, what you want, what are you going to tell me?' everything they don't want. Because they haven't learned how to think. Just like my boy said earlier, Sean.
They haven't spent any thinking time. So we walk them through that. Again, I'm going to get those numbers. Second thing. I'm going to schedule out one-to-one meetings biweekly. If you can do monthly, please do at least do one once a month. If you can't do bi-weekly, but I'm going to schedule these out. I repeat: I'm going to do what. Say it loud, come on. Listen, guys. If it's not on the schedule, it doesn't matter to you, and it will not happen. Okay. The truth is—The truth. I just know it to be true. If it's not on your calendar, it's not important. It will get overlooked. I am the best winger there is. Man, I can wing it. The amount of times someone called me and said, 'Hey, I need you to fill in for this gig.' So-and-so got sick.
And I pop off and I show up, you know, two days later, hop on a tour and knock out something. You know, I can wing it with the best of them. It is no way to do leadership. It's just not, it's not healthy, it's not going to actually give you the results that you're looking for. So, Third, I have a system for the one-to-ones. I make sure that every meeting flows the exact same way. There's nothing worse than having a meeting that is pointless. Can I get an amen on that? Come on, oh y'all, never y'all don't know. Amen, too many different religions in the room. Come on, let me get an amen. Come on, yeah. The consistent structure is actually going to give you space as a musician.
I used to think that when people put box parameters around me, it stopped my creativity. Again, remember, musicians aren't very smart. Poor guys and girls. The structure will actually give your team members space to become the best version of themselves. The structure will give the team member an opportunity to see what am i capable of, what am i actually made of. So that way, can I use you as an example? Okay, I know you already got slammed pretty hard. Mine's gonna be nice, be soft like a first-time lover. Okay. So in your case, how long have you been trying to hit X goal and you haven't done it yet? Right. It's been a minute. Because you haven't had that time to think about what do I really want?
What is my game plan to achieve that? How am I going to implement that? And then how do I move forward with that? It's the same thing that I do in a sales call. Hi. Here are the three problems I'm trying to solve when I was in a sales call. It's very simple. Do you have a problem? That I can solve. Are you open to getting help solving that problem? Are you qualified to get help solving that problem? That's it. Those are, that's what we teach. That's how we make tons of money. Because we keep it so simple, we break it down so basic, so that anybody can jump in and have an opportunity to change their life. I believe sales will change people's lives.
You want to make money very very very fast, outside of drugs, what else works? Okay, I'll sit it. Anybody? Say it loud. I was broke and struggling, guys. Broke and struggling. Sales literally changed my life. I dove into commercial real estate, I got, I got pieces on my arm, now I got freaking real commercial real estate around the United States. We got cowards and cowards and none of that really matters. It's just stuff that you get to buy because you need to buy something at the end of the day. I wasn't doing that on music. I did that on sales. So when I believe, and I hope y'all feel my conviction, sales will change your damn life.
And if you can get your team members to believe that, that's where you get that buy-in that is just beyond any type of result that you thought you could get. All right, let's keep moving. Okay. This is the one-to-one structure breakdown, okay? I break it down into basically four categories, okay? This is how we run a one-to-one. I actually have a form that we're gonna give away. See, whatever Jake says is what we do. We created a form. If you already have one, great. This is a Google sheet that we created. It looks kind of cool. Maybe it doesn't. I don't know. I think it looks cool, but it's a way that you can implement. These, you'll track it. Your team member tracks it, it logs it.
And then there's an actual data tracking system so that you can measure an in-measurable metric. Does that make sense? All right, cool. Game tape. First thing I do, I hop on the call. And I, or we, if it's a zoom, because a lot of our guys right now, it is a remote situation. I go through and I say, 'If it's the top of the month of a meeting, Hey, what'd you do last month? Give me the number.' I already know the numbers. Why do I want them to say it? Same reason I want a prospect to tell me why they can't get their ads to work. Because I want ownership and I want accountability. When you have ownership and you have accountability, you have a better chance of implementation.
Y'all with me? Okay. Oh, I did X amount of dollars last month. Fantastic. What did you want to do? What were you on pace to do? Did you fall short or did you exceed it? Great. What are your numbers for this month? Oh, I want to do X and S. Great. What's your game plan? How are you going to do that? Because we can show you down to a text message, we can show you down to a call— what it will take, actual work, actual effort, to achieve that said goal. Okay. So we walked through that. This is what we call game tape. Um, I live and breathe by this statement. A guy told it to me. Hmm. 2000 and it doesn't matter your long-term goals govern your short-term decisions.
Your long-term goals govern your short-term decisions. If I can get a sales professional Professional team member to simply say, 'I want X,' so no matter what I have to do this today. There's a guy that I and Jake are really good friends. He says it like this: 'This is who I am, so this is what I do.' I'm doing $1,000. I'm generating $500,000. I'm doing a million dollars a month. So when people are like, 'Well, why aren't you coming out to hang out?' No, I'm not slamming you, but why aren't you coming out to hang out? Oh, because this is who I am, so this is what I do. Well, why aren't you going out to hang when everyone goes and do that?
Oh, because I'm going to go and review a call because this is who I am. This is what I do. Does this make sense, guys? Is this like actually clicking or am I just, are you like, God, why do you let the new guy talk? Coaching, this is where I jump in and I coach them. So they tell me, 'Hey, they say da-da-da-da-da-da-da.' Now I'm going to coach them on what they say versus what I see to be true. Does that make sense? So we work through it. I don't slam them. I don't beat them up. I don't knock them down. I just simply say, you said you wanted A, B, and C, and D, and that you were going to do X, Y, and Z. But you didn't do that.
Why is that? I treat my one-to-ones the same way I treat my sales calls. I ask questions. Someone told me the other day, 'How do you know, Josh, how do you know what questions to ask on a sales call? How do you know what questions to ask your team members?' Because if I think it in my head and I assume. I learned one day, stop assuming, just ask a question. Oh, everything's going great. Yeah, you know, we're getting all the results that we're looking for. Oh, well, then why did you call me? Seems basic, don't it? I say this all the time. Sales is simple. You know how I know? Because I'm doing it. Right. But it's not easy. Sales is simple, but it is not easy.
So this is where I coach them. So again, I hit them, I review, I talk numbers, I talk metrics. Then I coach on hidden metrics, where they at, some things like— this allows them to set aside time once you get a month into this, two months into this, your sales team member will either a start showing up and being ready for these one-to-ones or they will bounce. I know that sounds harsh, but I'd rather— I don't know— I'm curious about this with you, my man. I'd rather have a culture where it demands excellence and greatness. Or they freaking quit. Do you align with that? Woo, what are you talking about tomorrow? You gonna hit on that? Oh my gosh, who's ready for Jordan? It's gonna be epic. He's great. He's got cards.
That's how they freaking get terrorists. I love it. Uh, game plan. Then I'm gonna craft a game plan that basically helps the team member establish clear expectations and encourage them to buy into their work towards their objectives. What's it going to take? To achieve X. When they leave that meeting, I want them to know I gotta make X dials, send X emails, I gotta hit this prospect up, seven days to close, 30 days to close. Days to close, I got to hit all them up. That's my game plan. This is how my week structured out. It's all listed inside of my Google Calendar. They're marked as free, so somebody could inbound could book over. I want to teach people how to make their money. If marketing turned off tomorrow, I love asking that question.
How are you gonna make your money if you ain't got no marketing? Because I don't know if you remember this time before 2000 and 2000. Sales have been just fine before Facebook ads. Am I wrong? Has civilization not lived off of sales since the beginning of time without ads? So how can we leverage what I call the traditional, the old-school way of sales, and leverage things like marketing that all of these amazing things that we can do? How do we combine them together? I want to see that happen more in anybody's team. I don't care who's teaching it, sales or marketing. I want to see them start working as one because they actually are one. They really are because it's supposed to be a seamless process. Yo, I did this thing, boom.
Anybody, sports fans in here, or is it just me? Everybody's like, 'Okay, sports fans.' I love Bill Belichick. Anybody know his famous statement? Oh, let's go. I don't even know you. I'm so happy right now. Do your job. If I do my job as sales, I actually make marketing look good. If marketing does its job, it makes sales look good. But what if we stopped caring about ourselves and we started caring about each other? And what could we accomplish? I don't think these revenue goals would be as hard to hit. Guys, don't ever underestimate the power of unity. Never underestimate that power. Yeah, fourth one, hidden game. This is where I break down. Once I've talked sales, I've talked numbers, I've talked metrics, now I ask them, what are you going to do this month?
And I have notes. I've got my notes on what their goals are. One of my team members, I said, 'Hey, you're in jujitsu.' Obviously, you just crushed. Oh, he got the Rollie. This dude's been wanting a Rollie since I met him. He called me right before he purchased it and he said, 'Dude, what do you think I should do?' Well, let me see. Your ass is single. You live in a one-bedroom apartment. I cut your checks for $25,000 to $30,000 a month. You better go buy the damn Rolex. I don't know how much longer you're going to have to wait. What else are you waiting for? He's like, 'Yeah, you think?' I'm like, 'Yeah.' It's been on your freaking vision board for two years.
At some point, you just have to give the people the money. Then they give you the time piece. It's not that hard. It's not complicated. He went out and got it. I mean, it changed the game. But what did he do right there in that moment? He created a marker. Right? The Jewish people used to set up, you know, I'm not trying to get religious, but it's a great analogy. They would set up altars or pillars when something happened. Right? They talk about in the ancient text that they would set up a pillar when they crossed through the Jordan. Right? It was not to just commemorate. What they had just done, it was also meant as a reminder of what I'm capable of. This is why I want salespeople freaking buying trinkets.
It's not that I want them spending their money to waste it. I want them spending their money so that they can realize I am capable of this. I set a goal. I accomplished the goal. And here's the damn thing that represents that I did that thing. So that way. When they start falling short. And they start acting like, what's the word? Human. Yeah, y'all with me? No one's sleeping, right? When they start acting human, I can say, 'You remember those breadcrumbs? You remember when you bought that Rollie? You remember when you got that car? You remember when you took that trip? You remember I had one salesperson.' His one goal was to take five cats on a freaking yacht. You remember when you did that? Yeah, yeah, I do remember that.
Was that an accident? Well, no, I worked hard. Okay, do you want to work hard again? Yeah, yeah, I do. Yeah, I do. Okay, well, let's do that. Is that it? Yeah, that's it. That's actually it. Just work. I know this might be crazy in 2023, but being a lazy ass won't get you anywhere. I know, I know Biden's America is destroying us, but the truth is the truth. You know what I'm saying? And he goes, 'Well, how do you want it to work?' I go, 'I don't know. Could you figure that out?' And he goes, 'Yeah, I'll be right back.' And he goes, and he comes back, and then everything's perfect, and the world's amazing. So we're going to get you this sheet. That's my presentation. I hope that this was helpful for y'all. Because. At the end of the day, the only thing that matters, I truly believe this, if we could put people first, I think profit will flow endlessly. I'm not telling you to waste your time and energy and effort on something that's not going to yield you money. I'm telling you: you can have more control over the money that you're making if you can invest into your people, if you can invest in them and lift them up and encourage them.
You can get people to stay. I watched people stay at a freaking sinking ship on a damn Titanic. I was there with them putting duct tape on the freaking iceberg hole, rearranging the freaking furniture on the Titanic as it was going down. Why were we there? Because leadership. Kept saying, 'Nah, we'll be all right. We'll get there. We'll keep going.' People were still getting paid even when it was bad. That is the power of leadership. Now imagine if the business is healthy. Imagine if you're implementing all these things that Jeremy teaches you and Sean and Jordan and everybody here that's amazing. Imagine if you started implementing those things and you actually had a healthy business and you coupled that with amazing leadership, serving and loving on people. All right, that's my time. Thank you all so much. I appreciate y'all.
